stories.title: RECALL — TOMY Boon NURSH Baby Bottles — Choking Hazard (40,000 Units, Walmart)

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
🚨 RECALL: TOMY Boon NURSH baby bottles — ~40,000 units recalled for a choking hazard.

Walmart only, Nov 2025–May 2026. Item B11654. Stop use now.

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting [URL]

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
⚠️ Boon NURSH 8 oz bottle (item B11654) — stop using it now.

Contact TOMY for a $22 store credit or replacement set.
Call 866-725-4407, M–F 8 AM–4 PM CT.

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting [URL]

article.headline: TOMY Boon NURSH Baby Bottle Recall: What COS Parents Need to Know

image_concepts.label: Image
image_concepts.raw:
TOMY Boon NURSH Baby Bottle Recall

**IMAGE 1-A** | Social (1200x675) | X/Twitter
Concept: A single pink baby bottle sitting on a clean white surface, soft studio lighting from above. The bottle shows subtle visual distress — the outer plastic shell has a faint bubbled, uneven texture visible near the top. Minimal, clinical still-life style. Color palette: soft pink and white with cool gray shadows. The bottom third of the frame is completely clear white surface — no objects. Art style: editorial product photography aesthetic, slightly desaturated.

**IMAGE 1-B** | Article Hero (1200x630)
Concept: Wide, low-angle shot of a baby bottle lying on its side on a white countertop. A parent's hand (from frame edge, adult, generic, no face visible) reaching toward it — a moment of concern and attention. Soft natural window light from the left. Color palette: clean whites and pale pinks, neutral skin tones. Art style: documentary lifestyle photography, warm but serious mood. Bottom-right third kept clear and uncluttered.

---

stories.title: AAP Releases Updated Drowning Prevention Guidelines — Layered Approach for Summer Safety

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text:
🌊 Drowning is the No. 1 cause of death for children ages 1–4 in the US.

Rates declined from 1999–2019. Now they're rising again.

AAP updated its drowning prevention guidelines in June 2026. #COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting [URL]

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text:
Summer swim safety for COS families — AAP 2026:

✓ Touch supervision (arm's length)
✓ Swim lessons after age 1
✓ 4-sided pool fencing
✓ Life jackets open water
✓ Adult CPR training

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting [URL]

article.headline: AAP 2026 Drowning Prevention Guidelines: What COS Families Need to Know This Summer

image_concepts.label: Image
image_concepts.raw:
AAP Drowning Prevention 2026 Guidelines

**IMAGE 2-A** | Social (1200x675) | X/Twitter
Concept: A young child (approximately age 2–3, generic appearance, no identifiable individual) wearing a bright orange Coast Guard-style life jacket, standing at the edge of a bright blue residential swimming pool on a sunny summer day. An adult's arm and hand are visible at frame left — showing close supervision within arm's reach. Sunlight sparkles on the water. Art style: authentic lifestyle photography, warm summer daylight. Color palette: vivid pool blue, orange life jacket, golden sunlight. Clean lower third — pool deck surface with no objects.

**IMAGE 2-B** | Article Hero (1200x630)
Concept: Wide shot of a residential backyard pool surrounded by four-sided white aluminum fencing, gate visible and closed, on a bright summer afternoon. A young child (age 3–5, viewed from behind at distance, not identifiable) stands near the fence looking at the water. Adult silhouette nearby. Art style: warm, realistic illustration or editorial lifestyle photography. Color palette: pool blue, green grass, white fence, soft afternoon sky. Composition keeps bottom right quarter uncluttered for text overlay.

---

stories.title: Pikes Peak or Bust Rodeo 2026 — Five Nights of Family Fun Starting July 14

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text:
🤠 Pikes Peak or Bust Rodeo is back July 14–18.

Evening shows 7:30 PM. Free 14-min drone show July 16. Pre-rodeo parade downtown COS July 11, 11 AM. NFR qualifying.

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting [URL]

article.headline: Pikes Peak or Bust Rodeo 2026: Family Guide to Colorado Springs' Best Summer Event

image_concepts.label: Image
image_concepts.raw:
Pikes Peak or Bust Rodeo 2026

**IMAGE 3-A** | Social (1200x675) | X/Twitter
Concept: A dramatic rodeo arena scene at night under bright stadium lights. A cowboy on horseback frozen mid-action in the ring, dust swirling from hooves. The crowd is blurred in the background. American flag visible on arena rail. Warm golden and amber tones from stadium lighting. Art style: high-contrast action photography aesthetic, moody and energetic. Color palette: golden amber, deep blue-black sky, white arena light. Clean lower third — dirt arena floor with no riders or objects.

**IMAGE 3-B** | Article Hero (1200x630)
Concept: Wide establishing shot of a rodeo arena at dusk, warm summer sky still glowing orange on the horizon. Stadium lights just beginning to illuminate the ring. Banners visible in background. Empty arena seats suggest pre-show atmosphere with excitement building. Pennants and flags catch a light breeze. Art style: editorial wide-angle photography. Color palette: warm dusk orange and gold, deep arena blues, rustic browns. No identifiable individuals. Right third of frame kept clear.

---

stories.title: Pikes Peak Sunrise Openings — Summer 2026 Schedule for Early-Morning Family Adventure

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
⛰️ Pikes Peak Sunrise Openings 2026 — next dates: July 12 and July 18.

Gateway opens 4:45 AM. $18/adult · $8/child · $65/carload. New shuttle option this year. Advance permits required.

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting [URL]

article.headline: Pikes Peak Sunrise Openings 2026: A COS Family Guide to America's Mountain at Dawn

image_concepts.label: Image
image_concepts.raw:
Pikes Peak Sunrise Openings

**IMAGE 4-A** | Social (1200x675) | X/Twitter
Concept: Pre-dawn view from a high alpine summit — rocky foreground terrain in silhouette, looking east across a vast mountain landscape. Deep purple and indigo sky above, the first hint of orange and gold sunrise bleeding across the horizon. Stars still faintly visible in upper sky. No people. Art style: dramatic landscape photography, high contrast, awe-inspiring. Color palette: deep purple-indigo sky grading to gold and orange horizon, black silhouette rock. Lower third is flat dark rocky foreground — clear for text overlay.

**IMAGE 4-B** | Article Hero (1200x630)
Concept: Wide panoramic sunrise view from an alpine mountain summit at approximately 14,000 feet. Layers of mountain ridges in purple-blue silhouette recede into the distance. Brilliant sunrise colors — vivid orange, gold, pink — dominate the right half of the frame. Thin wisps of cloud catch the light. No people, no structures. Art style: epic landscape photography, color-rich, cinematic. Color palette: vibrant sunrise oranges and golds, deep blue mountain silhouettes, pale sky gradient. Right quarter of frame kept light and clear.

---

stories.title: High-Altitude Sun Safety for COS Families — Summer UV Risk at Pikes Peak Country

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
☀️ COS sits at 6,035 ft — roughly 24% more UV than sea level.

SPF 30+ applied 30 min before going out, reapplied every 2 hours. Seek shade 10 AM–4 PM.

No sunscreen for babies under 6 months. #COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting [URL]

article.headline: High-Altitude Sun Safety for COS Families: What Parents Need to Know at 6,000+ Feet

image_concepts.label: Image
image_concepts.raw:
COS High-Altitude Sun Safety

**IMAGE 5-A** | Social (1200x675) | X/Twitter
Concept: A parent (adult, viewed from mid-torso, no face, generic) applying white sunscreen to a young child's (age 2–4) cheek and nose. They're seated outdoors on a sunny day. In the background, softly blurred, a red rock and pine tree landscape evokes Colorado mountain terrain. Bright, direct summer sunlight. Art style: warm authentic lifestyle photography. Color palette: warm skin tones, bright white sunscreen, vivid green and red-orange blurred background. Lower third: clear grassy or rocky ground with no objects.

**IMAGE 5-B** | Article Hero (1200x630)
Concept: Wide shot of a family hiking on a rocky alpine trail under intense bright sunshine. Three figures (adult + two children, viewed from behind, no faces, generic silhouettes) wearing wide-brimmed sun hats and light long-sleeve layers. Rocky terrain, blue sky, bright sun visible in upper corner. Pikes Peak-scale rugged landscape. Art style: lifestyle adventure photography, bright and sun-drenched, slightly high-key exposure. Color palette: warm golden sunlight, bleached-blue sky, tan and gray rocky trail. Right-lower quarter kept uncluttered for text overlay.

research.facts:
High-Altitude Sun Safety
| Fact | Confidence | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| Colorado Springs elevation: 6,035 feet | HIGH | coloradosprings.gov, official city sources |
| UV increases approximately 4% per 1,000 feet elevation | HIGH | skincancer.org, cancer.org (standard reference) |
| 6,035 ft = ~24% higher UV than sea level | MEDIUM | Calculated from 4%/1,000 ft rule; individual conditions vary |
| Garden of the Gods elevation ~6,400 feet | MEDIUM | General knowledge; exact elevation varies within park |
| Pikes Peak elevation 14,115 feet | HIGH | Well-established |
| No sunscreen needed for infants under 6 months — use clothing/shade/hats | HIGH | aap.org (AAP Healthy Children guidance) |
| Babies 6+ months: SPF 15–30 on exposed skin | HIGH | aap.org |
| SPF 30+ broad-spectrum, apply 30 min before, reapply every 2 hours | HIGH | aap.org, skincancer.org |
| UPF 50+ sun-protective clothing recommended | HIGH | aap.org |
| Seek shade 10 AM–4 PM peak UV hours | HIGH | aap.org, skincancer.org |
| Car windows block UVB but not UVA | HIGH | skincancer.org (standard automotive glass behavior) |
| UV penetrates clouds | HIGH | aap.org, skincancer.org |
